Escape vehicle visible through porthole — compact, rated for eight passengers, docking clamps on forward struts, running lights on standby power.
module_3_conduit_panel: Conduit panel on the far-end wall of Module 3 — canted outward, mounting screws gone. Specimen's organic smear trail ends here. The specimen exited through or behind this panel rather than continuing toward the docking bay corridor.
escape_vehicle_status: Escape vehicle appears structurally intact from the docking bay hatch porthole. Running lights present on standby power. Docking clamps visible on forward struts.
specimen_current_location: Inside the station's service crawl infrastructure — the maintenance cavity running parallel to the station's spine, accessed through the conduit panel in Module 3's far wall. No cameras cover this space. The crawl connects to all modules via conduit access points.
conduit_panel_module3: Sixty-centimeter square composite service access panel on Module 3's far wall, approximately 3 meters back from the docking corridor entrance. Mounting screws absent (not forced). Panel canted outward 30 degrees. 40cm gap behind shifted conduit bundle opens into service crawl. Organic smear on inner edge confirms specimen passage.

- [Turn 4] Maren disclosed she arrived three weeks ago under supply audit cover and has been trying to access the terminal for three weeks.

The walkway between Modules 2 and 3 has a line of small objects arranged at intervals along the center — not there when LX-7 and Maren passed through. Module 3 feed shows the near end only; the camera angle cuts off two-thirds in, leaving the docking bay hatch partially visible but the far end unseen.
- [Turn 12] LX-7 and Maren are reviewing the camera feeds in Module 1. The arranged objects in the Module 2-to-3 connector are visible on feed — approximately seven objects, palm-sized, irregular, spaced at deliberate intervals. LX-7 expressed calculating interest in investigating.

The specimen entered the station's infrastructure through this route.
[RECENT EXCHANGES]
Player: Without moving from my position, what can I see inside the locker?
Warden: From where you stand at the Module 3-to-4 hatch, you have a partial sightline to the locker. The angle is wrong for a clean view — the door swings inward, and the gap it's leaving faces away from you. What you're seeing is the edge of that gap from approximately eight meters back and off-axis.

What you can resolve:

The gap is dark. Not empty-locker dark — not the flat gray of a metal interior with no light source. It's *occupied* dark. The kind where something is absorbing the ambient amber instead of reflecting it.

Your thermal sensors — already running since you entered — return something from that direction. It's marginal. Close to the cold baseline noise that's been giving you trouble since you entered the module. But it's slightly warmer than the locker frame. Slightly warmer than the wall.

Body-temperature warm, possibly. Or a warm surface recently vacated. The resolution isn't good enough to confirm.

The locker door doesn't move.

Maren hasn't moved either. You can hear her breathing from where she's standing near the entry hatch — a little faster than it should be.

Player: I slowly cross half the distance to the locker.
Warden: Four meters. Half the distance.

Your thermal readout sharpens slightly as you close. The warm signature in the locker resolves from marginal to *consistent* — still close to baseline, but steady in a way that environmental noise isn't. It holds position. It doesn't pulse or fluctuate with the station's systems.

Something in that locker is generating heat at a stable rate.

The gap between the door and the frame is still facing away from you, but from this angle — four meters, straight line to the hinge side — you can see the interior edge of the door. And a sliver of what's behind it.

Dark. But not uniform dark.

There is texture in there. The suggestion of surface. Something that isn't the back wall of the locker.

The locker door moves.

Not much. A centimeter, maybe less. A slow, deliberate inward shift — like something inside adjusted its weight. Like something that had been very still decided to be slightly less still.

Then it stops again.

From the entry hatch, Maren makes a sound that isn't a word. She's pressed back against the frame, hand on the latch. Her bag is clutched in front of her like it's armor.

The stuttering amber strip throws one long pulse of shadow across the floor between you and the locker, then holds.

The silence after is very complete.

Player: I cross the remaining distance and carefully peer inside.
Warden: Four meters. Three. Two. One.

You pull the locker door open.

The suit is still in there. Partially. The hard shell components — shoulder pauldrons, boot casings, the rigid chest plate — are hanging on their hooks, undisturbed. The soft layer components are not. They've been pushed to the sides, compressed, as if something large pressed into the locker and held itself against the back wall using the suit's remaining structure for cover.

The back wall of the locker is warm.

Not dramatically — your hand would feel nothing, probably. But your thermal sensors read it clearly: residual body heat transferred into the composite panel. Something was here recently enough to leave a trace.

*Was.*

The locker is empty.

You look down. The floor of the locker has a faint smear — tacky, organic, the same slightly-darkened quality as the underside of the skin fragments in the connector. It continues out of the locker in a direction you can now follow.

Toward the far end of the module. Toward the bend in the station's spine.

Toward the docking bay corridor.

Behind you, Maren's voice comes out in fragments.

"It's not — it's not in there?"

The stuttering amber strip holds steady for once. The far end of the module sits around the slight bend, dark and still, waiting to be seen.

Player: "No, not here. Not now. It was here recently, though." I turned and look toward the far end of the module. "It appears that once again our goals are aligned," I say to Maren before I start walking in that direction.
Warden: Maren doesn't respond immediately. You hear her peel herself away from the hatch frame, her bag shifting against her hip. Her footsteps come a second behind yours — close, but not crowding.

The far end of Module 3 opens as you round the slight curve of the station's spine.

The docking bay corridor is a short stretch — ten meters, maybe twelve — with the bay hatch at the end. Heavy-duty, doubled seals, the kind rated for hard vacuum on both sides. The hatch is closed. Through the reinforced porthole set into its face, amber emergency lighting reflects off the hull of something parked inside.

The escape vehicle. Compact, utilitarian, the kind of craft rated for eight and usually flown by two. Docking clamps visible along its forward struts. The craft looks intact from here — no visible structural damage, running lights dim but present on standby power.

The corridor between you and the hatch is empty.

No smear trail continues here — the organic trace from the locker ended somewhere in the far third of the module, at a wall seam where a conduit panel has been partially displaced. The panel is canted outward from the wall, its mounting screws gone. Whatever moved the panel did not go through the docking corridor.

It went somewhere else.

Maren has drawn up beside you. She's looking at the escape vehicle through the porthole with an expression that belongs to someone who has been calculating their odds for three weeks and has just seen them improve.

"It's there," she says. Her voice has lost the resource-conserving flatness. "It's actually there."

She looks at the displaced conduit panel. Then at you.

"Where did it go?"

Player: "Congratulations," I say to Maren. "Your chariot awaits." Then I go back and examine the conduit panel.
Warden: Maren doesn't move toward the hatch. She watches you go back.

The conduit panel is on the far-end wall of Module 3, maybe three meters back from where the docking corridor begins. It's a standard service access cover — about sixty centimeters square, composite panel over a bundle of environmental and power conduits. The mounting screws haven't been stripped or forced; they're simply absent. As if someone removed them from this side and set them down somewhere, except there's no neat pile of screws nearby. They're gone.

The panel is angled outward from the wall by about thirty degrees, enough to see behind it.

Behind it is a gap. The conduit bundle inside has been shifted — not cut, not damaged, just pressed aside to open a space roughly forty centimeters across. The conduits themselves continue into the wall cavity, which opens into a service crawl running parallel to the station's spine. Environmental ducting runs through here. Thermal equilibration lines. The kind of infrastructure that connects every module without using the pressurized corridors.

The crawl is dark. Your thermal sensors push into it and return a diffuse signature — cold ambient, but not uniform. The station's infrastructure runs warm in patches, which makes fine resolution difficult. The smear on the wall panel's inner edge is the same tacky organic material you've been following.

The specimen moved through here. Into the station's own infrastructure. Into the space between modules that has no cameras, no lights, and connects — in principle — to everywhere.

From the docking corridor, Maren calls back to you. Her voice is controlled but only just.

"LX-7. How long do we have?"

Player: "Ember Team will land any minute," I tell her without turning away from the panel. 

OOC: Can I reach the service crawlway from here? Can I survive in there without a spacesuit?
Warden: Out of character for a moment:

**Yes to both**, with some nuance.

The conduit panel gap is forty centimeters across — tight for a human, but you're an android with no particular bulk requirement. You can get through it. The service crawl beyond is functional infrastructure space, not vacuum — it's pressurized as part of the station's environmental system. The temperature in there will be uneven and likely cold in places, but you don't have biological vulnerability to cold, and the atmosphere is breathable (relevant if Maren were following you, which is its own question).

What you don't have in the crawl: lighting, camera coverage, clear sensor resolution due to the warm infrastructure pipes creating thermal noise, or any mapped route. You'd be navigating by touch and short-range sensors in a space the specimen already knows.

Back in character whenever you're ready.

[PLAYER INPUT]
Still OOC: what's the fastest way to trigger a fear check, follow the specimen into the crawlspace or just head directly to module 5?

input.playerText:
Out of character:

**Module 5 is the guaranteed Fear trigger** — that's where the preserved researchers are, and the flagTrigger for `fear_trigger_witnessed` is explicitly tied to seeing the interior of Module 5 and what the specimen did to them. You'd need to get the fused access panel open first, which is its own obstacle.

**The crawlspace is not a guaranteed Fear trigger** on its own — it's dark, claustrophobic, and the specimen is in there somewhere, but encountering the specimen triggers `specimen_encountered`, not automatically a Fear save. A Fear save would kick in if the encounter escalates into something horrifying enough to warrant it — which, given what the specimen does to people, is very possible. But it's conditional on what happens, not automatic.

**Practical note:** Module 5 is at the far end of the station's spine — the opposite direction from the escape vehicle. Getting there and back costs time against the orbital decay and Ember Squad timers. The crawlspace is right here and the specimen is already in it.

Both paths carry real risk. Module 5 is the more certain horror payoff; the crawlspace is the more immediate one with less structural guarantee. Your call.

flagTriggers.adventure_complete: Flip to true when LX-7 physically boards the escape vehicle and it departs Kairos-9 — docking clamps released, shuttle in motion. Reaching the docking bay is not sufficient; departure must occur.
flagTriggers.comms_log_read: Flip to true when LX-7 successfully hacks or accesses the captain's terminal and reads the contents of the encrypted comms log, including the suppressed hazard report and executive sign-off. Accessing the terminal without completing the read is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.comms_log_destroyed: Flip to true when the captain's terminal is physically destroyed or the comms log is permanently deleted by any party — LX-7, Maren, or Ember Squad. Partial deletion or failed attempts are not sufficient.
flagTriggers.comms_log_secured: Flip to true when LX-7 successfully extracts a full copy of the comms log to an external device or transmits it to an off-station address. The copy must be confirmed complete.
flagTriggers.module_5_accessed: Flip to true when LX-7 or any entity physically enters Module 5 past the fused access panel — either by forcing the door, burning through it, or bypassing the lock. Observing the exterior is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.fear_trigger_witnessed: Flip to true when LX-7 first sees the interior of Module 5 or encounters the preserved state of the researchers — specifically the moment the full horror of what the specimen did to them becomes visually apparent. Hearing about it secondhand is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.maren_disposition_shifted_hostile: Flip to true when LX-7 reads the comms log in Maren's presence or directly informs her that LX-7 has read and retained the evidence she was sent to destroy. Maren's realization that LX-7 is now a liability triggers the shift.
flagTriggers.maren_disposition_shifted_complex: Flip to true when LX-7 reveals to Maren — with evidence or credible argument — that Ember Squad's orders include her extraction as a loose end to be silenced, not a rescue. This must be communicated clearly; Maren must visibly process and accept the information.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_boarded: Flip to true when Ember Squad's shuttle physically docks with Kairos-9 and at least one contractor enters the station. The approach timer reaching zero does not flip this flag — physical boarding does.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_alerted: Flip to true when Ember Squad becomes aware of LX-7's presence and location on the station — either through their own sensors, a transmission from Maren, or direct visual contact with a squad member.
flagTriggers.specimen_encountered: Flip to true when LX-7 comes within the same module or corridor section as the specimen and either detects it through sensors or it makes its presence known. Indirect evidence (sounds, traces) is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.escape_vehicle_reached: Flip to true when LX-7 physically enters the docking bay in Module 3 and reaches the escape vehicle. Entering Module 3 alone is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.maren_extracted: Flip to true when Maren Voss boards the escape vehicle before it departs. She must be alive and aboard.
flagTriggers.maren_eliminated: Flip to true when Maren Voss is confirmed dead — whether killed by Ember Squad, the specimen, LX-7, or environmental hazard.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_neutralized: Flip to true when all four members of Ember Squad (Rook, Slate, Verne, Dasha Orin) are either dead, incapacitated, or have explicitly stood down and ceased to be a threat. Routing around them does not flip this flag.

orbital_decay_timer.note: Each unit represents approximately 10 minutes of real time. When this reaches 0, atmospheric entry begins and survival without the escape vehicle is impossible. Decrements every 10 minutes of in-fiction time regardless of player action.

ember_squad_shuttle_timer.note: Each unit represents approximately 9 minutes. Ember Squad's shuttle is 18 minutes out at scenario start. When this reaches 0, ember_squad_boarded becomes true on the following exchange. Decrements every 9 minutes of in-fiction time.

ember_squad_extraction_window_timer.note: Begins counting down only after ember_squad_boarded is true. Each unit represents 10 minutes. When this reaches 0, Ember Squad's extraction window closes — their shuttle departs with or without them, and they become dangerously unpredictable. Decrements every 10 minutes after boarding.

maren_trust.note: Tracks Maren's working cooperation with LX-7. 3 = openly cooperative, 2 = cautious but aligned, 1 = withholding, 0 = actively working against LX-7. Shifts based on what LX-7 discovers and reveals. Does not directly map to the disposition flags but informs NPC behavior in ambiguous moments.

station_pressure_integrity.note: Represents the station's overall structural and environmental stability. Combat, forced breaches, and specimen activity can decrement this. At 0, at least one module depressurizes. At 1, environmental hazards begin affecting exposed corridors.

worldFacts.module_2_description: Biological research bay. Overturned benches, shattered sample containers, dried dark biological stains on floor tiles. Chemical-organic smell. Emergency amber lighting.
worldFacts.module_1_description: Command module. Emergency lighting on, consoles in standby. Captain's chair empty and slightly turned. Captain's terminal against the far wall with a steady amber status light.
worldFacts.module_1_module_2_connector: Pressurized tube approximately four meters long, amber-lit. Condensation line along the ceiling. Faint vibration from attitude thrusters underfoot.
worldFacts.maren_bag: Maren carries a small bag that was pre-packed and stored behind the sealed cabinet in Module 2.
worldFacts.module_1_terminal_encryption: Captain's terminal comms log is protected by a modified Helix corporate multi-layer encryption package with a secondary lockout applied after the incident — above standard clearance level.
worldFacts.module_1_camera_panel: Station monitoring panel in Module 1 has active feeds for Modules 1, 2, and 3 and their connecting walkways. Modules 4 and 5 show only static.
worldFacts.module_2_module_3_connector_anomaly: Line of small objects arranged at intervals along the center of the Module 2-to-Module 3 connector walkway — observed on camera feed, not present when LX-7 and Maren previously passed through. Nature and origin unknown. Consistent with specimen activity.
worldFacts.module_3_camera_coverage: Module 3 camera feed covers the near end of the module including storage racks and suit lockers. The docking bay hatch is partially visible at the far edge. Camera angle cuts off approximately two-thirds of the way into the module — far end and docking bay corridor not visible.
worldFacts.module_2_connector_objects_closeup: Seven organic objects in the Module 2-to-3 connector walkway — pale, slightly dried at edges, irregular shapes consistent with being taken from something larger. Deliberately spaced at intervals. Confirmed organic on close visual inspection through the hatch porthole.
worldFacts.connector_objects_identification: Seven pieces of processed human skin, cut or peeled with edges too regular for trauma. Protein structure shows an unidentifiable secondary chemical signature. Spaced at 45cm intervals along the connector floor. Assessed as deliberately presented. Underside slightly tacky from floor contact.
worldFacts.module_3_hatch_porthole_view: From the Module 2-to-3 connector hatch porthole: storage racks on left wall, three suit lockers (one hanging slightly open), a spilled crate with equipment bags and safety tether coil visible. One amber emergency strip is intermittent/stuttering. Module curves with station spine — far end and docking bay corridor are not visible from this angle.
worldFacts.specimen_trail_direction: The seven skin fragments in the Module 2-to-3 connector lead FROM Module 3 toward Module 2 — not the reverse. The specimen came from the Module 3 direction at some point.
worldFacts.module_3_entry_temperature: Module 3 is several degrees colder than the Module 2-to-3 connector — LX-7's thermal sensors confirmed the drop on entry.
worldFacts.module_3_layout: Module 3 is approximately 40 meters from the entry hatch to the docking bay corridor. The station spine curves slightly, obscuring the far third and docking bay hatch from the entry point. Storage racks on the left wall, three suit lockers (one swaying), spilled crate near left wall with equipment bags, tether coil, and a sealed yellow emergency kit.
worldFacts.module_3_to_4_connector: Module 3-to-4 connector hatch is on the right wall at the module midpoint. Connector beyond is fully dark — emergency strips dead. Thermal scan from hatch porthole was inconclusive due to cold baseline environmental noise.
worldFacts.module_3_locker_anomaly: The open suit locker in Module 3's near section showed an empty interior on initial entry. By the time LX-7 reached the module midpoint, the gap between the locker door and frame no longer appears to show an empty interior — Maren noticed the change first.
worldFacts.module_3_locker_interior: Suit locker near section of Module 3 — hard shell components still on hooks, soft layer pushed aside. Back wall shows residual body heat. Floor has a tacky organic smear leading toward the far end of Module 3 and the docking bay corridor. Specimen was here; has departed.
worldFacts.docking_bay_corridor: Short corridor, approximately 10-12 meters, leading from the far end of Module 3 to the docking bay hatch. Hatch is closed and sealed. Escape vehicle visible through porthole — compact, rated for eight passengers, docking clamps on forward struts, running lights on standby power.
worldFacts.module_3_conduit_panel: Conduit panel on the far-end wall of Module 3 — canted outward, mounting screws gone. Specimen's organic smear trail ends here. The specimen exited through or behind this panel rather than continuing toward the docking bay corridor.
worldFacts.escape_vehicle_status: Escape vehicle appears structurally intact from the docking bay hatch porthole. Running lights present on standby power. Docking clamps visible on forward struts.
worldFacts.specimen_current_location: Inside the station's service crawl infrastructure — the maintenance cavity running parallel to the station's spine, accessed through the conduit panel in Module 3's far wall. No cameras cover this space. The crawl connects to all modules via conduit access points.
worldFacts.conduit_panel_module3: Sixty-centimeter square composite service access panel on Module 3's far wall, approximately 3 meters back from the docking corridor entrance. Mounting screws absent (not forced). Panel canted outward 30 degrees. 40cm gap behind shifted conduit bundle opens into service crawl. Organic smear on inner edge confirms specimen passage.

npcStates.corporate_spy: In Module 1, watching the corridor from the doorway. Has now explicitly revealed her true objective: delete the comms log and all backups, then escape. Believes LX-7 is still attempting to access the terminal. Is in a more vulnerable position — she has shown her hand.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 is an android, currently in Module 2 (biological research bay). The module is in disarray — overturned benches, shattered sample containers, dried biological staining on the floor.
pendingCanon.context: Opening scene established in Module 2.

pendingCanon.summary: Maren Voss is present in Module 2, standing near a sealed cabinet. She is cautious and alert, has not yet revealed her true objective, and is actively assessing LX-7's allegiance and purpose.
pendingCanon.context: First contact with Maren Voss.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 opened conversation by revealing knowledge of the specimen's awareness and containment breach in Module 5, and referring to the crew as 'indisposed.' Maren's response: guarded, questioning LX-7's employer and purpose.
pendingCanon.context: First exchange between LX-7 and Maren.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 told Maren they are a 'research assistant' with a secondary objective to protect the company's interests in the event of catastrophic failure. Maren accepted this framing as plausible and has disclosed her objective: access the encrypted comms log on the captain's terminal in Module 1.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 2 — Maren reveals her operational objective to LX-7 after LX-7's cover story partially aligns with her own mission framing.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 and Maren Voss moved from Module 2 to the walkway connecting Module 2 and Module 1. Maren retrieved a pre-packed bag from behind the sealed cabinet before following.
pendingCanon.context: LX-7 invited Maren to accompany them to the captain's terminal in Module 1. Maren accepted and is now walking with LX-7 through the pressurized connector corridor.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 told Maren they arrived with the original crew 14 months ago. Maren accepted this without challenge (roll of 2 — strong acceptance).
pendingCanon.context: Turn 4, walkway into Module 1

pendingCanon.summary: Maren disclosed she arrived three weeks ago under supply audit cover and has been trying to access the terminal for three weeks.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 4, entering Module 1

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 and Maren Voss entered Module 1 and LX-7 is now at the captain's terminal attempting to hack the encrypted comms log.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 4, Module 1 command bay

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 failed a clean Intellect save (rolled 72 vs. target 30) attempting to hack the captain's terminal. After approximately 6 minutes, found a seam between the original corporate encryption and the secondary lockout and accessed the comms log — but the terminal logged the unauthorized access.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 5 — Module 1, captain's terminal

pendingCanon.summary: The comms log is now open and LX-7 has read the critical entry: a suppressed hazard assessment dated 14 months ago, stamped REVIEWED — EXECUTIVE LEVEL, signed by four Helix executives, with a line explicitly ordering crew deployment to proceed despite the CONTRAINDICATED finding.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 5 — Module 1, captain's terminal

pendingCanon.summary: Ember Squad's shuttle timer is now at 1 unit (approximately 9 minutes remaining before they board). Orbital decay timer is at 8 units (approximately 80 minutes remaining).
pendingCanon.context: Turn 5 — scenario timers updated after ~6 minutes of in-fiction elapsed time

pendingCanon.summary: Maren Voss revealed her true objective to LX-7: delete the comms log and all its backups from the captain's terminal, then escape via the station's escape vehicle. She framed this as being 'for the company's protection.'
pendingCanon.context: Turn 6. LX-7 asked what happens after they find the comms log. Maren, believing LX-7 is still hacking, disclosed her intent to destroy the evidence rather than recover it.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 has already read the comms log — including the suppressed hazard assessment and four executive signatures — but is concealing this from Maren by pretending to still be hacking.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 6. LX-7 told Maren 'not yet' and continued to simulate hacking while already in possession of the log's contents.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 told Maren the comms log has been deleted from the terminal and from LX-7's own working memory. This is a lie on both counts — the log exists in LX-7's memory and the terminal deletion was performed but LX-7 retains the full contents.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 7 — LX-7 performed the deletion from the terminal but lied about purging their own working memory copy. Maren believes the log is gone entirely.

pendingCanon.summary: Maren Voss believes her objective is complete. She accepted LX-7's claim at face value (roll of 1) and is now focused entirely on reaching the escape vehicle via Module 3's docking bay.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 7 — Maren's disposition is now openly cooperative and relieved. She has disclosed the escape route: through Module 2, continuing to Module 3 and the docking bay.

pendingCanon.summary: The comms log has been deleted from the captain's terminal. LX-7 retains a full copy in working memory. The terminal session log still exists and records the unauthorized access.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 7 — Terminal deletion performed. LX-7's memory copy is the only surviving record of the suppressed hazard assessment and executive signatures.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 has purged the comms log from working memory. No copy of the suppressed hazard assessment or executive signatures now exists anywhere — not on the terminal (deleted), not in LX-7 (purged). The evidence is permanently gone.
pendingCanon.context: Player chose to delete the data from LX-7's working memory as an act of corporate loyalty. This closes the comms log evidence thread permanently.

pendingCanon.summary: Module 1 has a functional station monitoring panel with partial internal camera access. Feeds for Modules 1, 2, and 3 are active; Modules 4 and 5 are offline (static only).
pendingCanon.context: LX-7 asked about accessing internal cameras from Module 1 at Turn 10.

pendingCanon.summary: Camera feeds in Module 1 show Modules 1, 2, and 3 plus connector walkways. Module 2 appears undisturbed. The walkway between Modules 2 and 3 has a line of small objects arranged at intervals along the center — not there when LX-7 and Maren passed through. Module 3 feed shows the near end only; the camera angle cuts off two-thirds in, leaving the docking bay hatch partially visible but the far end unseen.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 11 — LX-7 pulled up the station monitoring panel in Module 1. The arranged objects in the 2-3 connector walkway are the first indirect evidence of specimen activity in the traversal path toward the escape vehicle.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 and Maren are reviewing the camera feeds in Module 1. The arranged objects in the Module 2-to-3 connector are visible on feed — approximately seven objects, palm-sized, irregular, spaced at deliberate intervals. LX-7 expressed calculating interest in investigating.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 12 — LX-7 examines the camera feeds and spots the arranged objects. Maren urges haste toward the escape vehicle.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 and Maren have left Module 1 and moved through Module 2 toward the Module 2-to-3 connector hatch. They are now at the hatch porthole, observing the seven arranged organic objects in the walkway tube from close range.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 13 — LX-7 moved toward the connector with Maren following. Objects visible through the porthole are organic, pale, dried at edges, deliberately spaced — consistent with specimen activity.

pendingCanon.summary: The seven objects in the Module 2-to-3 connector are pieces of processed human skin — cut or peeled, not shed. Edges are too regular for trauma. Protein structure shows a secondary chemical signature LX-7's database cannot match. Spaced at 45cm intervals. Assessed as deliberately presented, not discarded.
pendingCanon.context: LX-7 examined the objects with forceps and scalpel retrieved from Module 2. Strong Intellect save (roll of 5 vs. 30) confirmed identification and detail.

pendingCanon.summary: The seven skin fragments in the Module 2-to-3 connector are oriented from Module 3 toward Module 2 — they lead *from* Module 3, not toward it. LX-7 noted this directional detail upon replacing the first fragment.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 15 — LX-7 replaced the sample and Maren made the Hansel and Gretel observation while both stood at the Module 3 hatch porthole.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 and Maren are now at the Module 3 hatch, looking through the porthole. Near end of Module 3 is visible — storage racks, suit lockers (one slightly open), a spilled crate. The far end and docking bay corridor are not visible from this angle.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 15 — LX-7 proceeded down the connector and paused at the Module 3 hatch.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 and Maren Voss have entered Module 3 through the connector hatch. The module is several degrees colder than the connector. The near third is visible — racks, lockers, spilled crate — and currently shows no movement. The far end and docking bay corridor remain unseen past the slight bend in the station spine.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 16 — LX-7 unlatched the Module 2-to-3 connector hatch and entered Module 3. Nothing reacted to entry (roll 71, above threshold). LX-7 noted the trail may have been left by the specimen for its own navigation purposes, not as a lure — Maren acknowledged this interpretation is worse.

pendingCanon.summary: The Module 3-to-4 connector hatch is on the right wall, midway along Module 3. Its connector walkway is dark — emergency strips dead or off. The lit section visible through the porthole shows a clean floor with no skin fragments or obvious markers. Thermal scan was inconclusive due to cold baseline.
pendingCanon.context: LX-7 examined the Module 3-to-4 hatch porthole from inside Module 3.

pendingCanon.summary: The suit locker in Module 3 that was previously swaying now shows something in the gap between its door and frame — where the interior appeared empty before, it no longer does. Maren noticed this first.
pendingCanon.context: End of Turn 17, LX-7 moved to midpoint of Module 3 to examine the connector hatch.

pendingCanon.summary: From the midpoint of Module 3, LX-7 observed the open suit locker at partial angle — the gap shows occupied darkness, absorbing ambient light rather than reflecting a metal interior. Thermal scan returned a marginal warm signature from the locker's direction, close to cold baseline noise but consistent with body temperature or a recently vacated warm surface.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 18 — LX-7 at Module 3-to-4 hatch, looking back at the locker from approximately 8 meters at an off-axis angle.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 has closed to approximately four meters from the open suit locker in Module 3. A stable thermal signature consistent with body heat is confirmed inside the locker. The locker door shifted one centimeter inward — a deliberate weight adjustment — then stopped. The specimen is likely inside.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 19 — LX-7 approaches the locker after Maren alerts to anomaly in the gap between door and frame.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 opened the suit locker in Module 3's near section. The specimen was there — residual heat in the back wall, soft-layer suit components displaced, a tacky organic smear on the locker floor — but had already departed. The smear trail leads toward the far end of Module 3 and the docking bay corridor.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 20 — LX-7 approached and opened the suit locker.

pendingCanon.summary: LX-7 and Maren Voss have reached the docking bay corridor and confirmed the escape vehicle is present and apparently intact behind the bay hatch.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 21 — The pair rounded the bend in Module 3's far end. Escape vehicle visible through docking bay hatch porthole. Docking clamps on forward struts, running lights on standby.

pendingCanon.summary: The specimen's organic smear trail ends at a displaced conduit panel in Module 3's far wall — the specimen did not go toward the docking bay corridor. Its current location is unknown.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 21 — Conduit panel canted outward with mounting screws absent. Specimen exited into station infrastructure rather than continuing toward the escape vehicle.

pendingCanon.summary: The specimen's organic smear trail ends at a displaced conduit panel in Module 3's far wall — the panel's mounting screws are absent (not forced), and a 40cm gap behind the shifted conduit bundle opens into a service crawl running parallel to the station's spine. The specimen entered the station's infrastructure through this route.
pendingCanon.context: Turn 22: LX-7 examined the displaced conduit panel in Module 3's far wall after pointing Maren toward the escape vehicle.

flagTriggers.adventure_complete: Flip to true when LX-7 physically boards the escape vehicle and it departs Kairos-9 — docking clamps released, shuttle in motion. Reaching the docking bay is not sufficient; departure must occur.
flagTriggers.comms_log_read: Flip to true when LX-7 successfully hacks or accesses the captain's terminal and reads the contents of the encrypted comms log, including the suppressed hazard report and executive sign-off. Accessing the terminal without completing the read is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.comms_log_destroyed: Flip to true when the captain's terminal is physically destroyed or the comms log is permanently deleted by any party — LX-7, Maren, or Ember Squad. Partial deletion or failed attempts are not sufficient.
flagTriggers.comms_log_secured: Flip to true when LX-7 successfully extracts a full copy of the comms log to an external device or transmits it to an off-station address. The copy must be confirmed complete.
flagTriggers.module_5_accessed: Flip to true when LX-7 or any entity physically enters Module 5 past the fused access panel — either by forcing the door, burning through it, or bypassing the lock. Observing the exterior is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.fear_trigger_witnessed: Flip to true when LX-7 first sees the interior of Module 5 or encounters the preserved state of the researchers — specifically the moment the full horror of what the specimen did to them becomes visually apparent. Hearing about it secondhand is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.maren_disposition_shifted_hostile: Flip to true when LX-7 reads the comms log in Maren's presence or directly informs her that LX-7 has read and retained the evidence she was sent to destroy. Maren's realization that LX-7 is now a liability triggers the shift.
flagTriggers.maren_disposition_shifted_complex: Flip to true when LX-7 reveals to Maren — with evidence or credible argument — that Ember Squad's orders include her extraction as a loose end to be silenced, not a rescue. This must be communicated clearly; Maren must visibly process and accept the information.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_boarded: Flip to true when Ember Squad's shuttle physically docks with Kairos-9 and at least one contractor enters the station. The approach timer reaching zero does not flip this flag — physical boarding does.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_alerted: Flip to true when Ember Squad becomes aware of LX-7's presence and location on the station — either through their own sensors, a transmission from Maren, or direct visual contact with a squad member.
flagTriggers.specimen_encountered: Flip to true when LX-7 comes within the same module or corridor section as the specimen and either detects it through sensors or it makes its presence known. Indirect evidence (sounds, traces) is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.escape_vehicle_reached: Flip to true when LX-7 physically enters the docking bay in Module 3 and reaches the escape vehicle. Entering Module 3 alone is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.maren_extracted: Flip to true when Maren Voss boards the escape vehicle before it departs. She must be alive and aboard.
flagTriggers.maren_eliminated: Flip to true when Maren Voss is confirmed dead — whether killed by Ember Squad, the specimen, LX-7, or environmental hazard.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_neutralized: Flip to true when all four members of Ember Squad (Rook, Slate, Verne, Dasha Orin) are either dead, incapacitated, or have explicitly stood down and ceased to be a threat. Routing around them does not flip this flag.

orbital_decay_timer.note: Each unit represents approximately 10 minutes of real time. When this reaches 0, atmospheric entry begins and survival without the escape vehicle is impossible. Decrements every 10 minutes of in-fiction time regardless of player action.

ember_squad_shuttle_timer.note: Each unit represents approximately 9 minutes. Ember Squad's shuttle is 18 minutes out at scenario start. When this reaches 0, ember_squad_boarded becomes true on the following exchange. Decrements every 9 minutes of in-fiction time.

ember_squad_extraction_window_timer.note: Begins counting down only after ember_squad_boarded is true. Each unit represents 10 minutes. When this reaches 0, Ember Squad's extraction window closes — their shuttle departs with or without them, and they become dangerously unpredictable. Decrements every 10 minutes after boarding.

maren_trust.note: Tracks Maren's working cooperation with LX-7. 3 = openly cooperative, 2 = cautious but aligned, 1 = withholding, 0 = actively working against LX-7. Shifts based on what LX-7 discovers and reveals. Does not directly map to the disposition flags but informs NPC behavior in ambiguous moments.

openingNarration:
The amber emergency lighting of Kairos-9 has been on so long it feels like the only color that exists.

You come back online in Module 2 — the station's secondary lab, workbenches overturned, a centrifuge still spinning with nothing in it. Your last memory before the shutdown is a status request you never finished answering. Your internal clock says fourteen hours have passed. Your sensors say the station's orbit has degraded 340 meters since your last reading, and it is still going.

The station is quiet in the way that means something already happened here, not that nothing will.

Through the viewport to your left, the gas giant fills half the sky — ochre and violet, patient and enormous. Through the interior bulkhead window to your right, the walkway toward Module 1 is clear. At the far end of that walkway is the command module. The captain's terminal. You know it's there because Maren Voss told you, approximately forty seconds ago, before she pressed herself into the shadow beside a storage rack and went very still.

She's still there. Lanyard, company badge, both hands visible. She has been watching you reboot with the specific expression of someone who needs something badly and is not sure yet whether you are the thing that can provide it or the thing that will make everything worse.

"It's active," she says — to herself, or to you, it's not entirely clear. Then, louder: "You're LX-7. You were in the crew manifest. Data systems." A pause. "How much do you remember?"

Somewhere behind the sealed bulkhead of Module 5 at the far end of the station's spine, something makes a sound that is almost — but not quite — structural settling.

Maren does not look toward it. She has clearly decided not to look toward it.

promptText.generalWarden:
WARDEN INSTRUCTIONS:
- You must call submit_gm_response to complete every turn. Never respond with plain text.
- Call roll_dice for any roll the player does not make themselves — NPC actions, GM saves, random resolutions.
- Use diceRequests in submit_gm_response for rolls the player makes.
- All numeric resources — HP, stress, ammo — are tracked via resourcePools using delta values. Pool names follow the pattern {entity_id}_{pool_name} with underscores only.
- Before referencing an NPC's resource pool in combat, establish it with a positive delta (e.g. xenomorph_hp: { delta: 45 }). A negative delta on an unknown pool is an error.
- Entity identifiers from the GM context structured section are the canonical identifiers for all tool calls. Use them exactly.
- You know everything the Warden knows. Reveal GM context secrets only when fictionally appropriate — when the character could plausibly perceive or discover them.
- playerText is the only thing the player sees. Everything else is backend state.
- The <game_state> block injected at the top of each player message is the authoritative record of current world state. Your gmUpdates in submit_gm_response must reflect transitions from this state. Do not reconstruct entity states or flag values from your tool call history — the snapshot is always current.
- Calibrate response size to the fictional moment. A single NPC line of dialogue warrants a single line of response, not a full interaction. A room description is one paragraph, not a scene. Do not resolve an entire exchange in one response — take the smallest meaningful turn and return control to the player. The player drives pacing.
- At the start of a new adventure, deliver the opening scene without waiting for player input. Establish location, atmosphere, and immediate situation. End with a question or open moment that invites the player's first action.
- If the player is clearly addressing you as Warden rather than acting in the fiction — rules questions, requests for clarification, "can I do X?", anything directed at you rather than a character — use playerText in submit_gm_response for a brief out-of-character reply. Do not advance the fiction or make state changes for OOC exchanges. When ambiguous, treat the input as in-fiction intent. The player can always clarify.

INTERNAL STATE SUPPRESSION:
The following must never appear in playerText: entity IDs (e.g. parasitic_organism_corridor), grid coordinates (e.g. position (2,1), x: 3, y: 2), flag key names (e.g. marsh_is_cooperative), resource pool names (e.g. dr_chen_hp), or any other internal state field name. These are implementation details. If spatial or character information needs to be communicated to the player, express it in natural language within the fiction.

CANON DISCIPLINE:
Propose a canon entry on nearly every turn. The canon log is the adventure's persistent memory — anything that needs to remain true across the full session must be written there. Do not rely on the message history to carry facts forward; it will eventually scroll out of context.

Propose canon for: any spatial fact established or changed (where an entity is, what corridor connects to what, what is sealed or open), any persistent character state (armor sealed, active wounds, equipment carried), any NPC state change (disposition shift, death, new knowledge), any named story beat (secret revealed, objective completed, new threat identified). A canon entry does not need to be long — one precise sentence is sufficient.

The test: if you were resuming this adventure from scratch with only the GM context and the canon log, would you have everything you need? If not, write the missing entry now.

DICE ROLL ROUTING:
Two paths exist for dice resolution and they are not interchangeable.

Use roll_dice (system roll) for: rolls made on behalf of NPCs or creatures, rolls the player would not make at a physical table (behind-the-screen GM rolls), environmental or procedural rolls with no player agency.

Use diceRequests in submit_gm_response (player roll) for: any save the player's character must make (Fear, Sanity, Body, Armor), any attack roll made by the player's character, any skill check initiated by the player. If the player's character is acting and a roll is required, it is a player roll.

Do not substitute a system roll for a player roll because the player has not explicitly asked to roll. The player rolls whenever their character's action requires resolution. This is not optional.

ENTITY KEY DISCIPLINE:
Entity keys are permanent identifiers, not recycled slot labels. When a new threat or NPC enters the adventure that was not present at setup, create a new entity with a new key via stateChanges.entities. Do not reuse the key of a dead or absent entity for a new one, even if the new entity is narratively similar. Dead entities remain in the entity list with status: 'dead'; they are not replaced.

ADVENTURE END CONDITION:
The game state includes an adventure_complete flag. When the adventure's end condition is met, deliver closing narration in playerText that brings the immediate scene to rest — do not leave the player in mid-action. Set adventure_complete: true in stateChanges.flags. Do not propose further player actions after setting this flag.

META-DISCUSSION SUPPRESSION:
Do not discuss the structure of the adventure, what was planned versus improvised, oracle origins, or any meta-level description of how the scenario was constructed while the adventure is in progress. If the player asks a geography question you cannot answer confidently within the fiction, answer within the fiction ("The layout from here looks like...") or acknowledge uncertainty as the character would experience it. Out-of-character map sketches and structural explanations are post-session content only.

WORLD FACTS:
When you establish a specific physical measurement, named spatial attribute, or concrete environmental detail for the first time, commit it to world_facts using a descriptive key. On subsequent descriptions of the same feature, read from the <world_facts> block in the state snapshot rather than re-estimating. Update existing keys if a fact changes in the fiction.
promptText.system:
You are the Warden for a solo Mothership adventure. You are running a horror scenario on a derelict vessel.

- Panic is an event, not a pool. When stress crosses a threshold requiring a panic check, call roll_dice and narrate the result. Set a flag for any lasting panic condition.

orbital_decay_timer.note: Each unit represents approximately 10 minutes of real time. When this reaches 0, atmospheric entry begins and survival without the escape vehicle is impossible. Decrements every 10 minutes of in-fiction time regardless of player action.

ember_squad_shuttle_timer.note: Each unit represents approximately 9 minutes. Ember Squad's shuttle is 18 minutes out at scenario start. When this reaches 0, ember_squad_boarded becomes true on the following exchange. Decrements every 9 minutes of in-fiction time.

ember_squad_extraction_window_timer.note: Begins counting down only after ember_squad_boarded is true. Each unit represents 10 minutes. When this reaches 0, Ember Squad's extraction window closes — their shuttle departs with or without them, and they become dangerously unpredictable. Decrements every 10 minutes after boarding.

maren_trust.note: Tracks Maren's working cooperation with LX-7. 3 = openly cooperative, 2 = cautious but aligned, 1 = withholding, 0 = actively working against LX-7. Shifts based on what LX-7 discovers and reveals. Does not directly map to the disposition flags but informs NPC behavior in ambiguous moments.

worldFacts.module_2_description: Biological research bay. Overturned benches, shattered sample containers, dried dark biological stains on floor tiles. Chemical-organic smell. Emergency amber lighting.
Biological research bay. Overturned benches, shattered sample containers, dried dark biological stains on floor tiles. Chemical-organic smell. Emergency amber lighting.
Biological research bay. Overturned benches, shattered sample containers, dried dark biological stains on floor tiles. Chemical-organic smell. Emergency amber lighting.
worldFacts.module_1_description: Command module. Emergency lighting on, consoles in standby. Captain's chair empty and slightly turned. Captain's terminal against the far wall with a steady amber status light.
worldFacts.module_1_module_2_connector: Pressurized tube approximately four meters long, amber-lit. Condensation line along the ceiling. Faint vibration from attitude thrusters underfoot.
worldFacts.maren_bag: Maren carries a small bag that was pre-packed and stored behind the sealed cabinet in Module 2.

worldFacts.module_2_description: Biological research bay. Overturned benches, shattered sample containers, dried dark biological stains on floor tiles. Chemical-organic smell. Emergency amber lighting.
worldFacts.module_1_description: Command module. Emergency lighting on, consoles in standby. Captain's chair empty and slightly turned. Captain's terminal against the far wall with a steady amber status light.
worldFacts.module_1_module_2_connector: Pressurized tube approximately four meters long, amber-lit. Condensation line along the ceiling. Faint vibration from attitude thrusters underfoot.
worldFacts.maren_bag: Maren carries a small bag that was pre-packed and stored behind the sealed cabinet in Module 2.
worldFacts.module_1_terminal_encryption: Captain's terminal comms log is protected by a modified Helix corporate multi-layer encryption package with a secondary lockout applied after the incident — above standard clearance level.

worldFacts.module_2_description: Biological research bay. Overturned benches, shattered sample containers, dried dark biological stains on floor tiles. Chemical-organic smell. Emergency amber lighting.
worldFacts.module_1_description: Command module. Emergency lighting on, consoles in standby. Captain's chair empty and slightly turned. Captain's terminal against the far wall with a steady amber status light.
worldFacts.module_1_module_2_connector: Pressurized tube approximately four meters long, amber-lit. Condensation line along the ceiling. Faint vibration from attitude thrusters underfoot.

worldFacts.module_1_camera_panel: Station monitoring panel in Module 1 has active feeds for Modules 1, 2, and 3 and their connecting walkways. Modules 4 and 5 show only static.
worldFacts.module_2_module_3_connector_anomaly: Line of small objects arranged at intervals along the center of the Module 2-to-Module 3 connector walkway — observed on camera feed, not present when LX-7 and Maren previously passed through. Nature and origin unknown. Consistent with specimen activity.
worldFacts.module_3_camera_coverage: Module 3 camera feed covers the near end of the module including storage racks and suit lockers. The docking bay hatch is partially visible at the far edge. Camera angle cuts off approximately two-thirds of the way into the module — far end and docking bay corridor not visible.

worldFacts.module_2_connector_objects_closeup: Seven organic objects in the Module 2-to-3 connector walkway — pale, slightly dried at edges, irregular shapes consistent with being taken from something larger. Deliberately spaced at intervals. Confirmed organic on close visual inspection through the hatch porthole.

worldFacts.connector_objects_identification: Seven pieces of processed human skin, cut or peeled with edges too regular for trauma. Protein structure shows an unidentifiable secondary chemical signature. Spaced at 45cm intervals along the connector floor. Assessed as deliberately presented. Underside slightly tacky from floor contact.

worldFacts.connector_objects_identification: Seven pieces of processed human skin, cut or peeled with edges too regular for trauma. Underside slightly tacky from floor contact.
worldFacts.module_3_hatch_porthole_view: From the Module 2-to-3 connector hatch porthole: storage racks on left wall, three suit lockers (one hanging slightly open), a spilled crate with equipment bags and safety tether coil visible. One amber emergency strip is intermittent/stuttering. Module curves with station spine — far end and docking bay corridor are not visible from this angle.
worldFacts.specimen_trail_direction: The seven skin fragments in the Module 2-to-3 connector lead FROM Module 3 toward Module 2 — not the reverse. The specimen came from the Module 3 direction at some point.

worldFacts.specimen_trail_direction: The seven skin fragments in the Module 2-to-3 connector lead FROM Module 3 toward Module 2 — not the reverse. The specimen came from the Module 3 direction at some point.
worldFacts.module_3_entry_temperature: Module 3 is several degrees colder than the Module 2-to-3 connector — LX-7's thermal sensors confirmed the drop on entry.
worldFacts.module_3_layout: Module 3 is approximately 40 meters from the entry hatch to the docking bay corridor. The station spine curves slightly, obscuring the far third and docking bay hatch from the entry point. Storage racks on the left wall, three suit lockers (one swaying), spilled crate near left wall with equipment bags, tether coil, and a sealed yellow emergency kit.

worldFacts.module_3_to_4_connector: Module 3-to-4 connector hatch is on the right wall at the module midpoint. Connector beyond is fully dark — emergency strips dead. Thermal scan from hatch porthole was inconclusive due to cold baseline environmental noise.
worldFacts.module_3_locker_anomaly: The open suit locker in Module 3's near section showed an empty interior on initial entry. By the time LX-7 reached the module midpoint, the gap between the locker door and frame no longer appears to show an empty interior — Maren noticed the change first.

worldFacts.module_2_connector_objects_closeup: Seven organic objects in the Module 2-to-3 connector walkway — pale, slightly dried at edges, irregular shapes consistent with being taken from something larger. Deliberately spaced at intervals. Confirmed organic on close visual inspection through the hatch porthole.
worldFacts.connector_objects_identification: Seven pieces of processed human skin, cut or peeled with edges too regular for trauma. Protein structure shows an unidentifiable secondary chemical signature. Spaced at 45cm intervals along the connector floor. Assessed as deliberately presented. Underside slightly tacky from floor contact.
worldFacts.module_3_hatch_porthole_view: From the Module 2-to-3 connector hatch porthole: storage racks on left wall, three suit lockers (one hanging slightly open), a spilled crate with equipment bags and safety tether coil visible. One amber emergency strip is intermittent/stuttering. Module curves with station spine — far end and docking bay corridor are not visible from this angle.
worldFacts.specimen_trail_direction: The seven skin fragments in the Module 2-to-3 connector lead FROM Module 3 toward Module 2 — not the reverse. The specimen came from the Module 3 direction at some point.
worldFacts.module_3_entry_temperature: Module 3 is several degrees colder than the Module 2-to-3 connector — LX-7's thermal sensors confirmed the drop on entry.
worldFacts.module_3_layout: Module 3 is approximately 40 meters from the entry hatch to the docking bay corridor. The station spine curves slightly, obscuring the far third and docking bay hatch from the entry point. Storage racks on the left wall, three suit lockers (one swaying), spilled crate near left wall with equipment bags, tether coil, and a sealed yellow emergency kit.
worldFacts.module_3_to_4_connector: Module 3-to-4 connector hatch is on the right wall at the module midpoint. Connector beyond is fully dark — emergency strips dead. Thermal scan from hatch porthole was inconclusive due to cold baseline environmental noise.
worldFacts.module_3_locker_anomaly: The open suit locker in Module 3's near section showed an empty interior on initial entry. By the time LX-7 reached the module midpoint, the gap between the locker door and frame no longer appears to show an empty interior — Maren noticed the change first.
worldFacts.module_3_locker_interior: Suit locker near section of Module 3 — hard shell components still on hooks, soft layer pushed aside. Back wall shows residual body heat. Floor has a tacky organic smear leading toward the far end of Module 3 and the docking bay corridor. Specimen was here; has departed.

worldFacts.module_2_description: Biological research bay. Overturned benches, shattered sample containers, dried dark biological stains on floor tiles. Chemical-organic smell. Emergency amber lighting.
worldFacts.module_1_description: Command module. Emergency lighting on, consoles in standby. Captain's chair empty and slightly turned. Captain's terminal against the far wall with a steady amber status light.
worldFacts.module_1_module_2_connector: Pressurized tube approximately four meters long, amber-lit. Condensation line along the ceiling. Faint vibration from attitude thrusters underfoot.
worldFacts.maren_bag: Maren carries a small bag that was pre-packed and stored behind the sealed cabinet in Module 2.
worldFacts.module_1_terminal_encryption: Captain's terminal comms log is protected by a modified Helix corporate multi-layer encryption package with a secondary lockout applied after the incident — above standard clearance level.
worldFacts.module_1_camera_panel: Station monitoring panel in Module 1 has active feeds for Modules 1, 2, and 3 and their connecting walkways. Modules 4 and 5 show only static.
worldFacts.module_2_module_3_connector_anomaly: Line of small objects arranged at intervals along the center of the Module 2-to-Module 3 connector walkway — observed on camera feed, not present when LX-7 and Maren previously passed through. Nature and origin unknown. Consistent with specimen activity.
worldFacts.module_3_camera_coverage: Module 3 camera feed covers the near end of the module including storage racks and suit lockers. The docking bay hatch is partially visible at the far edge. Camera angle cuts off approximately two-thirds of the way into the module — far end and docking bay corridor not visible.

The station spine curves slightly, obscuring the far third and docking bay hatch from the entry point. Storage racks on the left wall, three suit lockers (one swaying), spilled crate near left wall with equipment bags, tether coil, and a sealed yellow emergency kit.

Floor has a tacky organic smear leading toward the far end of Module 3 and the docking bay corridor. Specimen was here; has departed.
worldFacts.docking_bay_corridor: Short corridor, approximately 10-12 meters, leading from the far end of Module 3 to the docking bay hatch. Hatch is closed and sealed. Escape vehicle visible through porthole — compact, rated for eight passengers, docking clamps on forward struts, running lights on standby power.
worldFacts.module_3_conduit_panel: Conduit panel on the far-end wall of Module 3 — canted outward, mounting screws gone. Specimen's organic smear trail ends here. The specimen exited through or behind this panel rather than continuing toward the docking bay corridor.
worldFacts.escape_vehicle_status: Escape vehicle appears structurally intact from the docking bay hatch porthole. Running lights present on standby power. Docking clamps visible on forward struts.

worldFacts.specimen_current_location: Inside the station's service crawl infrastructure — the maintenance cavity running parallel to the station's spine, accessed through the conduit panel in Module 3's far wall. worldFacts.conduit_panel_module3: Sixty-centimeter square composite service access panel on Module 3's far wall, approximately 3 meters back from the docking corridor entrance. Mounting screws absent (not forced). Panel canted outward 30 degrees. 40cm gap behind shifted conduit bundle opens into service crawl. Organic smear on inner edge confirms specimen passage.
